So welcome to module 10 of straight line persuasion. Congratulations, you're here at the finish line. And what you're going to learn right now in this module is those last few strategies and techniques that will allow you to really get those final nuances that close everybody who's closeable. And the reason I said that is because remember that there are always going to be some people who are simply not closeable. And you're going to deal with those people with respect. You're not going to berate them. You're not going to harass them. You're going to deal with them on a level that makes you look like a professional because you are a professional and you're going to become a master persuader. Now, a couple of very important things here in the art of looping and the end game of sales. Remember that you are boxing somebody in. So it has to be done only for people that should be boxed in. You have to create that emotional case for buying as well as the logical case for buying. And as you're looping and going through the down the straight line, remember the scales. Remember the positives and the negatives. Remember that what you're really doing on a gut level here is you're knocking out the negatives, building up the positives and lowering an action threshold through language patterns. And ultimately what happens is this. It's like this and it's hovering and no result. And boom, the person buys. That's the bottom line. And I think in closing here, because this is so important, is I want you to focus on really your own strategy for what motivates you, what gets you going, what inspires you. Earlier on we spoke about vision. We spoke about your wise. We spoke about some very profound things that are going to really drive you past those sticking points, past those challenges that hold typical people back and force them into that box where they become creatures of circumstance. So now on a higher level, what I like to do is when you watch this video, focus on what I call the two specific anchors of motivation. You have on one side the pain anchor, which propels you from being in a box and from sitting still and not taking action. And then your anchor for pleasure, what pulls you towards what you want in life. Some people are motivated by pleasure, a lot more than pain. And this, in fact, more people who are motivated by pain. They won't do anything until April 15th in the morning when their tax is doing all of a sudden, wham, next thing you know, they're running in place, doing everything they've got to do because the pain of not doing their taxes has motivated them. So that's an ultimate truth in self-development. I didn't invent that. That's been going on for many, many years by some very profound people who've really broken that down into its essence. What I want here for you is I want you to actually take that now and internalize it. Use it in your own life. And here's how you're going to do it. You're going to identify what is ultimate pain for you. I want you to go through the exercise and list five things or five situations you might see yourself in that you consider ultimate pain. And by the way, it's going to be different for all of you. You know, what's my definition of ultimate pain might not be yours. Of course, there's a couple of things like, you know, being out in the street and everybody hating you, right? Those are ultimate pain for most of us. But I want you to dig deeper than that. I want you to find your ultimate pain on a why basis. What are the whys? Why is that ultimate pain for you? And I want you to write down your whys for ultimate pain. Then I want you on the other side to find out what's your ultimate pleasure? What's your vision? What's your feeling about ultimate pleasure? What are your whys? You know, how do you see yourself? And what is that would really drive you to be pulled towards wealth and success? And then what you're going to do, and you'll learn this on the last DVD module, what you're going to find here is that you're going to learn a technique that actually helps you move away from pain and pulls you towards pleasure. If you develop both of those anchors, one here and one here, you will find that you'll be able to move through those uncomfortable situations to overcome the specter of fear, to actually take fear and make it a powerful, motivating force versus this dastardly force that keeps you in a box. I'm a believer that while many people are scared of taking action because they think they're going to fail, so they don't want to be failure, so to speak, I believe that what holds people back from taking action, even more than fear of failure, is not truly believing that they're going to succeed. See, if I said to you right now, here's what you've got to do. You've got to take this action, that action, and that action, and I promise you, I'm going to give you a check for $1 million if you take those three actions. Well, I would guess that pretty much 100% of you would take those actions as long as you didn't see yourself jumping off a cliff and dying. You would take those actions, hopefully not hurting another human being in the process. But if you really, really knew that you would succeed, you take the action. If I said now, you've got a 50% chance of succeeding, well, I would see some of the hands in the group go down. If I said you had a 20% chance, more hands would go down. And when I get to a 2% chance, pretty much all the hands would go down. What I'm driving at is this. You've just learned an incredibly, incredibly powerful tool. It's a technique that I came up with in 1988 that allowed me to make hundreds of millions of dollars at an extraordinarily young age. It allowed me to train any person, and I said earlier, most of these people were not sales prototypes. They were not people whose parents instilled in them that they were capable of greatness. They were people who thought they'd end up maybe working in a 7-Eleven or just in a retail store. They were never thought they were capable of greatness. They were never told that. They weren't given those beliefs. So what I did is I helped them overcome those limiting beliefs by telling them the truth. And here's what it is. Every human being is capable of achieving greatness. Every human being is capable of knocking down barriers and obstacles that have held them back forever once they learn to harness the basic principles of pain and pleasure and to move through fear towards what they want, to not focus on what's wrong in their life, to focus on where they want to go. This is the way it works. Most of those are crap. You'll find out if they're real, if they're sticking to them. Remember? Okay. And here's the ticket, by the way. I want you to know this. Don't think, you know, I say the straight line is powerful. It is all the things I say it is. But in spite of that, you can't close everybody. And you shouldn't close everybody. Your goal is to close everybody who's closeable. And then take those people and turn them into customers for life. That's your goal. So how do you do that? Well, I'm going to give you a couple of ways here that are really powerful. First is never duck a phone call. Salesmen do this all the time. It is the worst thing you can ever do. You kill the relationship with one ducked call. Bad news, you give it to him. Don't forgive you. You make a bad move in marketing. If you act quickly, they will forgive you. Make a bad move in operation, they'll forgive you. Act quickly. You let it fester. You're done. They'll do business with anybody but you. You duck one phone call. I've had people that I've been business with doing business for years. They duck one call. I'm done. Unless I need them. If I don't need them, if I have a choice to do business with someone else, I'll do business with someone else. It's the worst thing you can do. Don't duck calls. And it also pisses your boss off, by the way, because he gets the call on compliance and complaints and so on and so forth. Send them to your competitor. If you really can't help your client, just send them to my competitor. Yes. Tell them where they can get the product that they need. If you do that, you have a customer for life. Next time, first of all, rave about you to all their friends and next time they need anything even remotely similar, they'll call you saying, well, if he doesn't know, I'm going to go get it. Amazing strategy. It also makes you feel good about yourself. This is good. It's good for you. Remember their family. Find out who they are after you've closed them. Don't go into the whole game to be their best friend before you've closed them. Time is money. But once they're your customer, get some basic facts. How do you do that? Azure closing this, Azure filling out the application, Azure, whatever. Quickly find out what their birthday is, what they have kids, what some of their interests are. We'll get to that in a second. Use gifts correctly. Now, more often than not, this is for a higher ticket of sale. Like if you're actually telling something to a big account or something, occasionally at the low level it can work too. But using gifts correctly, what that means is if you're out there giving out concert tickets to people or hockey tickets or baseball tickets, you better be sure that the person you even like likes baseball. I've seen, I've had more companies offer me tickets to baseball. He says, I hate baseball. Stupid part on the salesman. Now offer me tickets to a Rolling Stones concert. You got a customer for life. Get me a ticket to a tennis match. You have a customer for life. Find out what makes your customer feel alive outside of work and fill that need with your gift. Your customer loves fishing. Send them a magazine, Hunting and Fishing magazine. Every month, $1.50, he remembers you every single month for $12 a year. Something like that. Perfect example. Don't just send out random gifts to people. It's ridiculous. I get it. I almost turn like, what an idiot. The guy's like, stop it. Trying to bibribe me. But when I get something I love, I'm like, whoa, cool. I call it a thank you, man. Big difference. Right effective thank you notes. Language. Here's some key trigger words. I love, absolutely adore, the gift that you sent me. It looks amazing sitting on the centerpiece of my table for Thanksgiving. Someone sends you a piece. Don't just say thanks for the gift. It was really great. Be well. Sincerely, Jordan. Get personal. I loved it. I adored it. It was gorgeous. My family, people were commenting on it. That's how you write effective thank you notes. When someone gets that, they're moved emotionally. The other thing is every once in a while you get into a situation where you can give a really, really special gift to a big client and you got to go jump through Hoopsford. One of the stories I heard, which I wasn't done to me, it was just a great story, is a guy was trying to crack a big account at Ford and he couldn't get into Ford. It was a huge account to get into Ford. He actually went out and found out that the sales, the person that controlled the buyer had a son who loved a specific sports player and he went out and got a signed football from the athlete and then brought the signed football and had delivered to the kid directly. The guy that made like $60 million in sales and had been buying from Ford, he got for a football, cost him $50. That's powerful. Don't underestimate the ability of a very simple $50 gift to get you massive, massive credibility. Massive. Everyone follow the concept? This is you. We spoke a lot about motivation and stuff like that. Let me just tell you what I would say to my brokers way back in the day. I was doing something without even knowing it that was really clever. I was using NLP on them, NeuroLinguistic Programming and I didn't even know I was doing it because a lot of what NLP is, it's just very basic common sense, the still down to its essence. It's almost like the straight line. There's nothing so earth-shatteringly, like it's a revolutionary, but it's just so perfectly orchestrated that it distills down sales to its best. The motivation strategy that I gave to my brokers was as follows. Each morning, I would give a meeting for about an hour and each afternoon I'd give a meeting for two hours for like five years straight. Then I'd give meetings for four or five hours once a week at night time. That's how they got trained so well with the straight line. And it also trained me on the straight line by having to speak about this stuff all the time. What I'd say to them is this. I'd say the moment you step through this door and sit behind the desk and pick up a phone and say the words I teach you, everything in your past falls off. You become as powerful as the most powerful CEO in America. This phone is the great equalizer. It's the great equalizer when you say the words the way I teach you with the right tone and the right structure. As a matter of fact, you came from Harvard or Hell's Kitchen. You would come great, wildly successful with this little black telephone. That's what I would say to them. I'd say act as if. So at the beginning of the film, I'd say act as if you're a wealthy man rich already and you'll become wealthy. Act as if you have all the confidence in the world and people will have confidence in you. Act as if you have all the answers and the answers will come to you. Act as if. I don't have to act the physiology of success because you generate the emotion that way, the state that way, and it allows you to access the resources, the tones, the certainty. Those positive empowering states, the 3C's, when you're in a positive peak state, you can commit miracles. You simply can. And I would say to them now, if you don't want to do this, this is not for you fair enough. Get the hell out. And I didn't say it so nicely. But before you leave, take a good look at the person sitting to your left because one day in the not so distant future, you're going to pull up to a red light and they'll be sitting in a beautiful Ferrari. And next to it will be a beautiful girl. Absolutely gorgeous. And what kind of car will you be in? A beat up piece of shit pinto with some ugly woman who's bitch slapping you when she's in a bad mood. When you're done flipping burgers there and you're 65 years old trying to live on a pension which will be worthless and social security will be bankrupt and you're in that nursing home being fed so straw by someone named Polly who bitch slapped you when she's in a bad mood. I'm saying this for a reason. Saying it for a reason. Much of what I said was not true. There are other ways to make money to work at my firm. What I was doing was clever on my part. I didn't even know I was creating a strong anchor, a propulsion system. Watch. I want to show you this because I want you to do this for yourself in an ethical way that empowers you. What I did is I created a vision of them. It was so awful. So awful. And they're like visual and I would go through this again. I really was good at it. I would get them like they'd be like they'd be laughing and they would cry. They'd go, hey, nervous laughter. Oh, not me. But inside they were dying. The trees from the price club in the back seat. You don't even know what your life is going to be like. And I watched them melt and wilt in the seats. What I was doing and they know what I was doing, it just sounded good to me at the time. I was creating what's called a propulsion system. This is pain. I was giving them something to move away from. If you don't roll up your sleeves and go to work, that's what you're going to face. And they're like, so they work. I motivated them by pain. Great visual, but some of that's not really true. It's not quite ethical. It's a little bit off kilter. My moments were impure. But what can we learn from that is this. That is called gravitational pull for pleasure. So every single day, I push them away from pain and pull them towards pleasure. That's called a motivation strategy. Everybody is motivated differently. Some people move away from pain. You tell them how bad it's going to be if they don't take action and they act. Other people couldn't care. They don't care about that. They don't motivate them. Those people move towards pleasure. Tell them how wonderful it will be if they paint the picture here and they'll move towards it. They get pulled towards pleasure. Pushed away from pain, propulsion pulled towards pleasure. Most human beings have a bit of both. And there's no right or wrong. It's just how we are. I surprisingly move away from pain. I also get pulled towards pressure, but I move away from pain really, really well. If I have any hint that I might fail, and I failed many, many times, I will work and work and work and work. Who's like that? Raise your hand if you like that. A lot of us like that. Who here likes to move towards pleasure? You see something great you got there? Either way, I have both. What I do is I know how I'm built and I create in my mind. I use it to my advantage to run my own psychology. I create anchors to move away from to pull me towards. I have visions. I have my goal sheet and I get vision above it. My vision of what my future is going to be. And I let it pull me. My vision. And I have this box, this awful place that I know exists. That there will be some people out there in the world, maybe some people that are very close to you, that really, not that they don't want to see you succeed, but they're almost scared of your succeeding. I call these people the dream stompers. And these are the people that they stomp on your hopes and your dreams for a better life. The naysayers, the doubting Thomas, the people who will say, oh, that won't work. Or you trust the guy, oh, please, oh, please, oh, please, oh, please. And let me just tell you something. There's only one way to deal with dream stompers, to get them out of your life. I want you to lose their phone numbers, not forever, but at least 90 days. And by the way, it would be better to be forever. And if it just so happens that someone very close to you is a dream stomper, whether it be your spouse or someone, your parents, I want you to ask them to hold off on the dream stomping, not forever, but give you at least 30 days of no dream stomping to support you for the first month. And you say, you know what, listen, every time you want to stop on my dreams, just write it down. And then in 31 days, you can kill me with it. But at least let them give you 30 days here, because what's going to happen is this. I spoke earlier about the snowball effect. You put one piece of the puzzle into place and another piece of the puzzle into place. And all of a sudden, one builds on the other and you get that downward momentum rolling down there like the snowball. And it just explodes.
